A shop owner lends a pot to a neighbor. The neighbor mistakes the leftover liquid for ordinary broth, but it actually contains a truth tied to mistaken identity.

Overnight, the little shop in the south of town suddenly closed. The next morning, the owner stood at the doorway, pale.

He told the police: The pot is still here, but the soup is gone. Everyone else thought it was absurd, but only the person who asked about the pot knew why he kept staring at the kitchen corner.

What was in the pot was not ordinary food, but a medium for storing information.
The real key is the time gap, not the theft itself.
The neighbor and the owner understand the same object differently.
Smell, temperature, or residue will determine the truth.
The owner used the pot to store evidence tied to an old case. After the neighbor borrowed it
